entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'Lectures on Condensed Mathematics', Peter Scholze (joint work with Dustin Clausen), submitted 5 May 2026; abstract confirms it is the 'stable citable version' of the 2019 Bonn course, matching the bib note verbatim.
entries.explanation: Cited three times as the foundational reference for condensed sets, the sheaf condition on profinite sets, and full faithfulness of X -> cond{X} on compact Hausdorff/compactly generated spaces. All three uses accurately reflect this source's content.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; a well-established, long-standing arXiv paper (Barwick & Haine, 'Pyknotic objects I', 2019) consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited once alongside clausen-scholze-condensed as a joint foundational reference for the sheaf-theoretic definition of condensed/pyknotic sets. Pyknotic objects are the parallel, essentially-equivalent formalism, so the pairing is accurate.

entries.notes: No arXiv ID (lecture course, not a preprint) -- the bib entry flags this correctly. Not independently re-fetched via WebFetch this session; the MPIM/IHES 'Analytic Stacks' course (2023-2024) is a well-documented, publicly known course consistent with the citation.
entries.explanation: Cited three times: for the definition of light condensed sets/light profinite probes, the 'separable => light' clause of Prop. condcstar's proof, and as the stack technology conj:substack's closedness claim would need. All three uses match this course's actual subject matter (light condensed abelian groups, analytic rings, analytic stacks).

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'Classification of gapped ground state phases in quantum spin systems', Yoshiko Ogata, ICM 2022 proceedings; abstract confirms the operator-algebraic approach.
entries.explanation: Cited twice: for the general claim that ground states of physical interest are states on the quasi-local algebra, and for split-property/superselection background in conj:split. Both uses are consistent with this survey's scope; the fetched abstract excerpt did not itself use the phrase 'split property', but this is standard, expected content of Ogata's operator-algebraic classification program and not a surprising attribution.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; Nachtergaele-Sims-Young bulk-gap stability is a well-established, frequently-cited result consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited once alongside ogata-classification-review as background for 'the ground states of physical interest are states on qla'. Accurate, generic supporting citation.

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'Automorphic Equivalence within Gapped Phases of Quantum Lattice Systems', Bachmann/Michalakis/Nachtergaele/Sims, Comm. Math. Phys. 309 (2012) 835-871. Abstract confirms spectral-flow automorphisms generated by quasi-adiabatic evolution connecting gapped ground states in the same phase, satisfying a Lieb-Robinson bound -- matching the paper's proof of Theorem thm:groundstate closely.
entries.explanation: One of the two citations specifically flagged for verification. The paper's proof states the BMNS theorem 'produces the quasi-adiabatic spectral-flow cocycle: a norm-continuous family of *-automorphisms alpha_s ... with omega_s = omega_0 . alpha_s'; the verified abstract's own language matches this characterization. No misattribution found.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; Hastings-Wen quasi-adiabatic continuation (2005) is a foundational, extremely well-known result consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited alongside bmns-automorphic in the same proof for the quasi-adiabatic continuation technique underlying the spectral-flow construction. Accurate pairing -- Hastings-Wen originates the method BMNS builds on.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; well-known Nachtergaele-Sims-Young quasi-locality bounds paper, consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited alongside paperLocality for the claim that the spectral-flow generator is quasi-local with Lieb-Robinson-controlled tails, giving norm-continuity of the automorphism orbit on local observables. Accurate, on-topic citation.

entries.notes: Internal companion paper (Part I of this series). Confirmed a corresponding source file exists in the project: papers/latex/lieb-robinson-locality.tex.
entries.explanation: Cited multiple times as the source of the interaction Banach space B_F, the Heisenberg-dynamics morphism, and the Lieb-Robinson quasi-locality machinery Theorem thm:groundstate leans on. Standard, accurate internal cross-reference for a coordinated modular series.

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'Periodic table for topological insulators and superconductors', Alexei Kitaev. WebFetch described the venue as the L.D. Landau Memorial Conference proceedings (Chernogolovka, 2008); this conference's proceedings were published as AIP Conf. Proc. 1134 (2009), which is exactly the bib's journal listing -- no real discrepancy, just two descriptions of the same publication.
entries.explanation: Cited for K-theoretic classification/tenfold-way background, both in the main text and in cor:condinvariant's proof. Correct, canonical attribution -- this is the paper that introduced the periodic table.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; well-known Thiang K-theoretic classification paper, consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited three times for K-theoretic classification of the periodic table, alongside kitaev-periodic-table and prodan-sb. Accurate, standard attribution.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; the Prodan-Schulz-Baldes Springer monograph is the standard textbook reference for K-theory of topological insulators, consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited four times (tenfold-way background, SSH Toeplitz-index computation, cor:condinvariant's proof, conj:real discussion, and the gap-labelling remark) -- appropriate given this is a comprehensive monograph covering all of these topics.

entries.notes: Classic, extremely well-known 1979 PRL; predates arXiv, so a DOI-only citation is expected and correct.
entries.explanation: Cited once to introduce the SSH chain (ex:ssh) -- the canonical original reference. Correct.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; well-known K-theoretic bulk-edge correspondence paper, consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited for the SSH Toeplitz-index/operator-K claim and for the bulk-boundary remark realizing the boundary invariant via the Toeplitz extension's connecting map. Both uses are precisely on-topic.

entries.notes: Classic, extremely well-known 1982 PRL (TKNN); predates arXiv, so a DOI-only citation is expected and correct.
entries.explanation: Cited once for the Chern-insulator Hall-conductance quantization claim. Correct, canonical attribution.

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'Lectures on Analytic Geometry', Peter Scholze (joint work with Dustin Clausen), submitted 5 May 2026, confirmed stable citable version of the 2019/20 Bonn course. However, it is cited as the sole source for 'the solidification functor Solid ... reflection onto solid condensed spectra of [this]'; solid abelian groups (the base case of solidification) are more commonly first introduced in the companion Condensed Mathematics notes (already cited separately as clausen-scholze-condensed), with Analytic Geometry generalizing to the broader analytic-ring framework.
entries.explanation: Downgraded from high to medium relevance only on this attribution-precision point, not because the source is inauthentic or off-topic -- both Clausen-Scholze courses are genuine and legitimately discuss solidity. Not a misattribution, but arguably the less precise of the two possible citations for this specific clause.

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title '(Semi)topological K-theory via solidification', Ko Aoki, submitted 2 Sep 2024. Abstract: 'We demonstrate that the solidification of algebraic K-theory recovers two known constructions: the semitopological K-theory of a real (associative) algebra and the topological (aka operator) K-theory of a real Banach algebra.' This is an almost verbatim match to the paper's Theorem thm:aoki statement.
entries.explanation: The single most load-bearing external citation in the paper (Theorem II-B), specifically flagged for verification. The match between the verified abstract and the paper's theorem statement is exact on both clauses (semitopological K-theory of a real algebra; topological/operator K-theory of a real Banach algebra), including the real-algebra hypothesis the paper is careful to flag. No misattribution; this is the strongest-supported citation in the paper.

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'The Non-Commutative Geometry of the Quantum Hall Effect', Bellissard/van Elst/Schulz-Baldes; abstract confirms the NCG-of-QHE framework proving quantized/plateaued Hall conductivity. The retrieved abstract excerpt did not itself use the phrase 'gap-labelling', but this is the definitive, universally-cited source for exactly that construction (K-theory of the crossed product / trace-K0 pairing), consistent with domain knowledge and with the paper's own citation cluster (paired with kellendonk-tilings and prodan-sb for the same claim).
entries.explanation: Cited for the crossed-product-algebra / gap-labelling-group claim in Theorem thm:crossed's proof. Correct, canonical attribution for Bellissard's crossed-product formalism.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; well-known Kellendonk tilings/gap-labelling paper, consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited alongside bellissard-ncg-qhe and prodan-sb for the gap-labelling group claim. Accurate, standard attribution -- Kellendonk's paper is a primary source for gap-labelling via noncommutative geometry of tilings.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; consistent with the bib entry (Kubota, 'Controlled Topological Phases and Bulk-edge Correspondence').
entries.explanation: Cited once for 'the coarse/Roe-algebraic picture gives the same invariants a controlled-topology description' -- an exact match to this paper's own title and subject.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; title and theme ('Topological phases of non-interacting systems: a general approach based on states') are consistent with the bib entry and with the confirmed abstract of the companion paper denittis-rendel-weyl (an overlapping-author sequel work).
entries.explanation: Cited alongside denittis-rendel-weyl for the state-space/bundle-of-pure-states approach to phase classification in conj:classifying. Accurate, on-topic pairing.

entries.notes: Externally verified via WebFetch: title 'A scheme for topological phases of the Weyl C*-algebra', De Nittis & Rendel, submitted 1 Jul 2026 (revised 3 Jul 2026). Abstract confirms classification by 'homotopy classes of sections of certain fiber bundles of (pure) states', recovering K-theoretic classification for types A and AI -- an exact match to the paper's characterization.
entries.explanation: A very recent (2607.xxxxx = July 2026) posting, flagged for verification given the paper's own 14 July 2026 dateline; confirmed genuine and accurately described. No misattribution.

entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; well-known Ogata H^3(G,T)-index paper, consistent with the bib entry.
entries.explanation: Cited alongside ogata-classification-review for the split-property/index-construction claim in conj:split. Accurate -- Ogata's H^3 index construction is a primary example of an operator-algebraic SPT index building on the split property.

entries.notes: Internal companion paper (Part III of this series). Confirmed a corresponding source file exists in the project: papers/latex/spectral-gap-stability.tex.
entries.explanation: Cited for the uniform-gap hypothesis of Theorem thm:groundstate and the gapped locus U_f this paper's ground-state section restricts to. Standard, accurate internal cross-reference.

entries.notes: Internal companion paper (Part IV of this series). Confirmed a corresponding source file exists in the project: papers/latex/lattice-eft-equivalence.tex.
entries.explanation: Cited for the group-completion into the invertible spectrum IP^cond that the Aoki bridge feeds. Standard, accurate internal cross-reference.

entries.notes: Internal companion paper (Part V of this series). Confirmed a corresponding source file exists in the project: papers/latex/bordism-realizability.tex.
entries.explanation: Cited for the functorial-realizability question over disorder hulls that Theorem thm:crossed and conj:solidnat feed into. Standard, accurate internal cross-reference.

entries.notes: Internal companion paper (Part VI, the synthesis paper). No corresponding source file was found yet in papers/latex/ as of this review (2026-07-14); per the project's own task tracker the synthesis paper is still pending in the production pipeline. This is a forward reference within a coordinated, actively-produced modular series (the other five parts already exist as files), not a broken or fabricated citation.
entries.explanation: Cited once in the companion-papers section as the paper that 'assembles the modules'. Appropriate framing given the series' own stated structure; worth re-checking once Part VI is drafted.

missing_references: Glimm, J. G., 'On a certain class of operator algebras,' Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 95 (1960) -- the classical origin of uniformly hyperfinite ('Glimm') algebras. Prop. prop:uhf derives the UHF/AF structure from scratch and is correct without it, but does not cite the source of the construction/terminology it names.

Bratteli, O., 'Inductive limits of finite dimensional C*-algebras,' Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 171 (1972) -- the foundational classification paper for AF algebras; relevant background for Prop. prop:uhf's AF claim, currently uncited.

A direct citation to Weibel for semitopological K-theory: Theorem thm:aoki explicitly names 'Weibel's semitopological K-theory of A' but only cites Aoki's paper, which itself builds on Weibel's construction. A direct reference (e.g. Weibel's K-book or the relevant homotopy K-theory paper) would remove one step of indirection from that specific attribution.
summary: All 27 unique \cite keys used in the paper resolve cleanly in references.bib, and the embedded thebibliography reproduces the identical 27-key set with matching author/title/venue/arXiv details -- no orphan citations and no unused bibitems. Eight externally-loaded claims were spot-verified via WebFetch against arxiv.org, including the two highest-risk, most load-bearing citations the paper's theorems depend on: Aoki's solidification theorem (arXiv:2409.01462), whose abstract matches Theorem II-B's statement almost verbatim, and the Bachmann-Michalakis-Nachtergaele-Sims automorphic-equivalence theorem (arXiv:1102.0842), whose content matches the proof of Theorem thm:groundstate closely. Both 2605.xxxxx Clausen-Scholze lecture-note IDs (Condensed Mathematics, Analytic Geometry) and the 2607.xxxxx De Nittis-Rendel Weyl-algebra ID were confirmed as genuine, correctly-dated 2026 postings whose abstracts match their cited context exactly. No misattribution was found anywhere in the citation surface. The five internal companion-paper citations (Parts I, III, IV, V, VI) were checked against the project's actual files: four correspond to existing .tex sources, and the fifth (Part VI, synthesis) is a forward reference to a companion paper still in the production pipeline -- expected for a coordinated modular series, not a defect. One minor, low-severity attribution-precision note: the citation for 'the solidification functor' points solely to the Analytic Geometry notes, where the more commonly-cited original source for solid abelian groups is the companion Condensed Mathematics notes (both are genuine and on-topic, so this is a precision quibble, not an error). A few classical background citations (Glimm/Bratteli for UHF-AF theory, a direct Weibel citation for semitopological K-theory) are absent but not required, since the paper proves the relevant operator-algebra facts directly and the Aoki citation already carries the semitopological-K-theory attribution.
